result:
**The Peculiar Art of Noticing**

I've been thinking lately about attention—not the kind we seek from others, but the kind we pay to the world. It strikes me that we live in an era simultaneously drowning in information and starved of true observation. We document everything and notice nothing. Our cameras capture thousands of sunset photos that we'll never revisit, while the actual sensation of watching daylight fade—the way shadows lengthen like fingers reaching across grass, the particular quality of golden hour that makes even mundane buildings look ecclesiastical—slips by unregistered.

There's a woman who walks past my window every morning at 7:43. I don't know her name, but I know she has three different winter coats that she rotates seemingly at random, unbound by weather logic. The red one appears on both bitter cold days and mild ones. She always pauses at the same crack in the sidewalk—not to avoid it, but as if it marks some invisible boundary where she allows herself to check her phone. This tiny ritual, repeated daily, has become a kind of punctuation mark in my mornings.

Would I have noticed her before I started deliberately paying attention? Probably not. She would have been just another figure in the urban landscape, no more significant than a mailbox or a stop sign. But the act of noticing has transformed her into something else—a character in the ongoing narrative of this particular street, this specific coordinate point on Earth.

The practice of deep noticing feels almost rebellious now. Our economy runs on captured attention, harvested and monetized by algorithms designed to make us scroll rather than see. We're encouraged to consume experiences rather than digest them. Even our language has shifted—we "grab" coffee, "catch" up with friends, "steal" moments—as if time and experience were thieves we're trying to outrun rather than rivers we're standing in.

I've started keeping what I call a "noticing journal"—different from a regular diary in that it's not about my feelings or events, but about tiny observations that would otherwise evaporate. The way ice forms on puddles in hexagonal patterns, like nature attempting mathematics. The fact that my neighbor's cat sits in the same window but faces different directions depending on the day of the week (Tuesdays: always looking left). The gradual migration of a coffee stain on the café ceiling that no one else seems to track but which has moved three inches westward over six months.

These observations are useless in any practical sense. They won't improve my productivity, advance my career, or make me more attractive on dating apps. But they've done something else—they've made the world feel more inhabited. Not just by me, but by the full weight of existence pressing down on every square inch of reality, demanding witness.

Scientists talk about "plant blindness"—the human tendency to overlook plants in our environment, to see them as backdrop rather than beings. I think we suffer from everything blindness. We walk through miracles daily and call them mundane. The fact that water falls from the sky and we've named it something as pedestrian as "rain." The way strangers synchronize their walking speeds on busy sidewalks without negotiation, creating spontaneous rivers of movement. The bizarre reality that we've all agreed to stop at red lights—a collective fiction made real through repetition.

Children are master noticers. They haven't yet learned what's supposed to be important, so everything has equal weight. A broken stick is as fascinating as a smartphone. A puddle reflects the entire sky. Adults find this exhausting—"Come on, we need to go"—but what if the child is right? What if the ant carrying a crumb really is as significant as the meeting we're rushing toward?

I'm not advocating for some kind of return to primitive wonder where we spend hours contemplating leaves. We have responsibilities, obligations, the machinery of living that requires maintenance. But I do think there's space—even just five minutes a day—to practice seeing what's actually there rather than what we expect to be there.

The payoff is subtle but profound. The world becomes less generic. Your neighborhood stops being "the place where you live" and becomes a collection of specific trees that bloom in a particular order each spring. You start to notice that the barista at your coffee shop has been teaching herself to write people's names in increasingly elaborate scripts, a tiny art project playing out on disposable cups. You realize that the elderly man at the bus stop isn't just waiting but is conducting some kind of informal survey, greeting every dog that passes.

This kind of attention is different from mindfulness, which often feels like work—another task to optimize, another way to improve yourself. Noticing is more like play. It's the difference between meditation and daydreaming, between studying a map and wandering until you're lost.

As I write this, the light is changing outside. Not dramatically—it's overcast, has been all day. But even within that gray, there are shifts. The clouds have texture, depth, movement. They're not just "clouds" but specific formations with their own personalities, their own trajectories. Somewhere above them, the sun is tracking across the sky, invisible but still painting shadows.

The woman with three coats will pass by again tomorrow at 7:43. The coffee stain will continue its western migration. The world will keep offering itself up for observation, indifferent to whether anyone accepts the invitation. But for those who do, who practice the peculiar art of noticing, life becomes less like something we're getting through and more like something we're attending.
